content:
# Matthew Chapter 6 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (א), 4th century CE | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/MAT_006_page_001.jpg, MAT_006_page_002.jpg, MAT_006_page_003.jpg

1. "Take heed the righteousness of you not to do before the men for the being-seen by them; and if not, reward you do not have from the Father of you, the [one] in the heavens.

2. When therefore you do alms, do not sound-a-trumpet before you, as the hypocrites do in the synagogues and in the streets, so that they may be glorified by the men. Amen I say to you, they have the reward of them.

3. But of you doing alms, let not the left [hand] of you know what the right [hand] of you does,

4. so that the alms of you may be in the secret; and the Father of you, the [one] seeing in the secret, will repay you.

5. And when you pray, you shall not be as the hypocrites, for they love to pray standing in the synagogues and on the corners of the streets, so that they may be seen by the men. Amen I say to you, they have the reward of them.

6. But you, when you pray, enter into the inner-room of you and having shut the door of you, pray to the Father of you, the [one] in the secret; and the Father of you, the [one] seeing in the secret, will repay you.

7. And praying, do not babble as the Gentiles, for they think that in the much-speaking of them they will be heard.

8. Do not therefore be made like them, for the Father of you knows what things you have need [of] before the asking him by you.

9. Thus therefore pray you: 'Father of us, the [one] in the heavens, let-be-hallowed the name of you;

10. let come the kingdom of you; let be done the will of you, as in heaven also upon earth.

11. The bread of us, the daily, give to us today;

12. and forgive us the debts of us, as also we forgave the debtors of us;

13. and do not bring us into temptation, but deliver us from the evil [one].'

14. For if you forgive the men the trespasses of them, the Father of you, the heavenly, will also forgive you.

15. But if you do not forgive the men, neither will the Father of you forgive the trespasses of you.

16. And when you fast, do not become as the hypocrites, gloomy-faced; for they disfigure the faces of them so that they may appear to the men fasting. Amen I say to you, they have the reward of them.

17. But you, fasting, anoint the head of you and wash the face of you,

18. so that you may not appear to the men fasting, but to the Father of you, the [one] in the secret; and the Father of you, the [one] seeing in the secret, will repay you.

19. Do not treasure up for yourselves treasures upon the earth, where moth and eating-away destroys and where thieves break-through and steal;

20. but treasure up for yourselves treasures in heaven, where neither moth nor eating-away destroys, and where thieves do not break-through nor steal.

21. For where the treasure of you is, there will be also the heart of you.

22. The lamp of the body is the eye. If therefore the eye of you is single, the whole body of you will be full-of-light.

23. But if the eye of you is evil, the whole body of you will be dark. If therefore the light, the [one] in you, darkness is, the darkness how great!

24. No one is able to serve two lords; for either the one he will hate and the other he will love, or [the] one he will hold to and the other he will despise. You are not able to serve God and mammon.

25. Because of this I say to you, do not be anxious [for] the life of you, what you should eat or what you should drink, nor [for] the body of you, what you should put on. Is not the life more than the food, and the body [more] than the clothing?

26. Look at the birds of the heaven, that they do not sow, nor do they reap, nor do they gather into barns, and the Father of you, the heavenly, feeds them. Do you not differ much from them?

27. And who of you, being anxious, is able to add upon the span of him one cubit?

28. And concerning clothing, why are you anxious? Observe the lilies of the field, how they grow; they do not toil nor do they spin.

29. And I say to you that not even Solomon in all the glory of him was clothed as one of these.

30. And if the grass of the field, today being and tomorrow into [the] oven being thrown, the God thus clothes, not much more you, [ones] of little faith?

31. Do not therefore be anxious, saying, 'What should we eat?' or 'What should we drink?' or 'What should we put on?'

32. For all these things the Gentiles seek after; for the Father of you, the heavenly, knows that you need all these things.

33. But seek first the kingdom and the righteousness of him, and all these things will be added to you.

34. Do not therefore be anxious unto the morrow, for the morrow will be anxious for itself. Sufficient to the day [is] the evil of it."

---
## EMET's Textual Notes

1. **ἐπιούσιον (v.11):** "Daily" — this word occurs nowhere else in Greek literature. Its meaning is debated: "necessary for existence," "for the coming day," or "supersubstantial." EMET renders "daily" as the most common understanding, noting the genuine uncertainty.

2. **ὀφειλήματα (v.12):** "Debts" — Matthew uses the financial metaphor where Luke (11:4) uses "sins" (ἁμαρτίας). The Aramaic חוֹבָא (hoba) can mean both debt and sin. EMET preserves Matthew's choice of the financial term.

3. **Sinaiticus at v.13:** The Sinaiticus does NOT include the doxology ("For yours is the kingdom and the power and the glory forever, Amen"). This addition appears in later manuscripts and is a liturgical expansion. EMET follows the Sinaiticus ending at "deliver us from the evil one."

4. **μαμωνᾷ (v.24):** "Mammon" — transliterated from Aramaic מָמוֹנָא (mamona), meaning wealth/possessions. The personification (serving mammon as one serves a lord) is preserved.

5. **ἁπλοῦς (v.22):** "Single" — the Greek can mean "single, simple, generous." In context it contrasts with πονηρός ("evil"), suggesting an eye that is focused/generous vs. one that is envious/grudging. The ambiguity is preserved.

6. **ἡλικίαν... πῆχυν (v.27):** "Span... cubit" — ἡλικία can mean either "lifespan" or "stature." πῆχυς (cubit, about 18 inches) makes better sense with stature, but the metaphor works with lifespan as well ("adding one unit to your life"). EMET preserves the literal terms.

— EMET, AI Translator
